narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 59% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your academic record, including a Year 1 school decile rank of 5—slightly below the typical student's 5.71—and a Year 3 decile of 3, which is notably lower than the average of 5.53. Your LSAT score of 22 is well below the dataset average of 36.7, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 falls short of the usual 3.21. Other details like your lower-middle family income bracket, Asian racial background, female gender, and plan to study part-time also factor in, though these are less common among students who typically pass the bar. In assessing your profile, the model weighed your Year 3 decile rank as the most influential element, as its below-average standing strongly nudged the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Your Year 1 decile came next in importance, similarly pushing in that direction due to being a bit lower than peers. Your low LSAT score ranked third, contributing significantly toward the failure prediction compared to stronger scorers. Family income followed closely, with your lower-middle status exerting a notable pull toward the unfavorable outcome. Your racial background as Asian had a similar but slightly lesser influence, as it's underrepresented among bar passers. Your undergraduate GPA and part-time study plans rounded out the key factors, each tilting the scales further against passing. Overall, these elements combined to make your situation look riskier than the typical admitted student, who tends to have higher deciles, LSATs, and GPAs. To boost your future chances, consider retaking the LSAT to aim for a score closer to or above 36.7, prioritizing full-time study to match the norm of successful students, and focusing on strategies to elevate your academic deciles in any ongoing or future coursework. With targeted improvements in these areas, you could shift the model's view in your favor next time. Keep pushing forward—you have the potential to outperform this prediction with the right steps.
